comments.author: @EdgeTalesDocs
comments.text: This documentary is absolutely breathtaking—it beautifully captures the soul, history, and resilience of Bosnia in a way I've never seen before. The cinematography, especially of the primeval forests and the reconstructed bridge at Mostar, was stunning. It’s a powerful reminder of how important it is to look past the headlines and truly see the heart-shaped land at the center of Europe.

replies.author: @trueunrealglobe
replies.text: I enjoy how every destination is introduced with its own story. It makes each location memorable long after the documentary has ended.

comments.author: @Edith-e3n6
comments.text: Almost all European countries never existed before the 19th century. Europe was always multicultural and made of mixed culture city states. The "returning the homeland" stuff is 19th century national romanticism that has killed and displaced millions.

comments.author: @World_Untold_321
comments.text: Having visited Mostar and Sarajevo a couple of years ago, I can confirm every word in this documentary captures the soul of Bosnia perfectly. Drinking traditional Bosnian coffee by the Baščaršija and watching the divers on the Stari Most are core memories I’ll cherish forever. The resilience, warmth, and hospitality of the Bosnian people are truly unmatched despite everything they’ve been through. Thank you for making such a respectful and breathtaking film!

comments.author: @nedzad-h6r
comments.text:
Incredible! Just last night, I posted a suggestion on this channel asking for a follow-up video about South Korea—a beautiful country I was fortunate enough to visit. Today, I had the absolute pleasure of watching this video, which presents the history of my homeland, Bosnia and Herzegovina, in such an objective, thoughtful, and heartwarming way.

Yes, Bosnia has existed through various forms of statehood for over a thousand years—from a medieval Banate to today's internationally recognized country. What I deeply appreciate is how objectively you highlighted the undeniable historical fact that three religions coexisted in Bosnia long before the arrival of the Ottoman Empire. There was a fully independent and often challenged Bosnian Church—a true thorn in the side of both Rome and Constantinople back then. There were also the Bogomils (Patarenes), historically known as "Good Bosnians," a people who reclaimed their identity and are known today as Bosniaks.

For over 500 years, our Sephardic Jewish neighbors (who arrived from Spain) have lived alongside us. In the most profound way, they helped shape the social, political, and cultural identity of Sarajevo and Bosnia. Sadly, after the Holocaust, their numbers decreased significantly. Today, a small but vibrant Jewish community of around a thousand members lives in Sarajevo—highly educated, progressive, and above all, wonderful neighbors.

Centuries of peaceful coexistence were shattered in an instant in April 1992, when the war in Bosnia and Herzegovina and the Siege of Sarajevo began. Having survived the absolute hell of the siege myself, I still carry deep and visible scars from that horrific war.

The war ended in 1995 with the signing of the Dayton Peace Agreement. While it stopped the fighting, it brought a heavy and incredibly complex peace. As a lawyer, I used to have frequent discussions with my respected Constitutional Law professor (who also served as the President of the Constitutional Court of BiH before he passed away). He told me that despite studying political systems for over 40 years and holding top academic honors, he had never encountered a system as complicated, expensive, and inefficient as the current one in Bosnia. For a population under 4 million, we have 14 separate governments with over 250 ministers across different administrative levels, along with two entities and the Brčko District.

Currently, there is no consensus among our three constituent peoples and citizens on how to reform this structure. Put simply, my country desperately needs a European-style constitution, as well as EU and NATO membership. Just as peace was imposed in 1995, the international community needs to help introduce a new Constitution that works fairly for all three peoples, our 25 registered national minorities, and all citizens.

Yet, it is not all grim. My country is breathtakingly beautiful, and tourism is growing rapidly. The two most visited cities are Sarajevo and Mostar, welcoming travelers from all over the world who discover something genuinely authentic and fascinating here.

For visiting tourists, let me share a simple explanation of our country's name:

Bosna refers to the northern (continental) region, named after the Bosna River. Its source, Vrelo Bosne, is located in the suburbs of Sarajevo and is a popular nature park and tourist spot.

Herzegovina refers to the southern (Mediterranean) region, named after a medieval ruler (Herceg). The largest city in Herzegovina—and an absolute must-visit—is the gorgeous city of Mostar.

Thank you to everyone who takes the time to watch this beautiful, yet bittersweet story about my beloved homeland. Welcome to Bosnia and Herzegovina!

replies.author: @nedzad-h6r
replies.text: @Earthcue Thank you for your kind words. You really should visit Bosnia, though. In terms of history, architecture, culture, and tradition, Sarajevo is a fascinating blend of Istanbul and Vienna. A special recommendation for everyone: visit Sarajevo during the Sarajevo Film Festival. Those are magical moments in the city. The festival is officially ranked fourth in Europe—right behind Venice, Cannes, and Berlin. All the best!

replies.author: @Earthcue
replies.text: This comment alone is worth more than any travel guide about Bosnia. Thank you for sharing your story 🙏
